words.exampleEN: On King's Day everyone wears orange.

words.audioHint: Three syllables. A beloved Dutch tradition for children.
Two syllables. 'Kerst' relates to 'Christ'. The Dutch celebrate two Christmas days.

words.exampleNL: In Nederland heeft bijna iedereen een fiets.
words.exampleEN: In the Netherlands almost everyone has a bicycle.
words.audioHint: The 'ie' sounds like 'ee' in 'feet'. Cycling is a way of life in NL.

words.exampleNL: De gezelligheid in Nederland is bijzonder.
words.exampleEN: The coziness/togetherness in the Netherlands is special.
words.audioHint: Four syllables. A core Dutch cultural concept with no exact English translation.

words.exampleNL: Nederland heeft veel polders onder zeeniveau.
words.exampleEN: The Netherlands has many polders below sea level.
words.audioHint: Two syllables. Land reclaimed from the sea. A Dutch engineering feat.

words.exampleNL: Een stroopwafel is lekker bij de koffie.
words.exampleEN: A syrup waffle is nice with coffee.

words.exampleNL: De tulpen bloeien in het voorjaar.
words.audioHint: One syllable. The 'u' is short. Symbol of the Netherlands.

words.exampleNL: De molens van Kinderdijk zijn wereldberoemd.
words.exampleEN: The windmills of Kinderdijk are world-famous.
words.audioHint: Two syllables. Long 'o' in the first syllable. Iconic Dutch symbol.
'nieuw' (new) + 'jaar' (year). Dutch New Year includes fireworks everywhere.

words.exampleNL: Vrijheid is een belangrijke Nederlandse waarde.
words.exampleEN: Freedom is an important Dutch value.
words.audioHint: Two syllables. 'vrij' means free, '-heid' is a suffix like English '-dom'.

words.exampleNL: Het Nederlands voetbalelftal speelt in het oranje.
words.exampleEN: The Dutch football team plays in orange.
words.audioHint: The 'oe' sounds like 'oo' in 'food'. The national sport of the Netherlands.
